column2: The standard reference posture: standing upright, facing forward, arms at sides, palms turned forward.
column4: All directional terms assume the body is in anatomical position, even if the patient is lying down.

column2: The hierarchy from chemical to cellular, tissue, organ, organ system, and organism.
column4: The levels of organization let you say whether a word names a cell, a tissue, an organ, or a whole system.

column2: A vertical plane that divides the body into left and right portions.
column4: A sagittal plane cuts the body lengthwise into left and right sides.

column2: The sagittal plane that passes through the midline, producing equal left and right halves.
column4: A midsagittal plane splits the body into mirror-image halves.

column2: A vertical plane that divides the body into anterior (front) and posterior (back) portions; also called the coronal plane.
column4: A frontal plane separates the chest from the back.

column2: A horizontal plane that divides the body into superior (upper) and inferior (lower) portions; also called the axial or horizontal plane.
column4: Most CT scan images are taken in the transverse plane, as if the body were sliced like a loaf of bread.

column2: The posterior body cavity, which contains the cranial cavity (brain) and the spinal cavity (spinal cord).
column4: The brain and spinal cord sit inside the dorsal cavity.

column2: The anterior body cavity, which contains the thoracic cavity above the diaphragm and the abdominopelvic cavity below it.
column4: The ventral cavity is divided by the diaphragm into a thoracic cavity and an abdominopelvic cavity.

column2: A thin double-layered membrane that lines a ventral cavity and covers its organs; the pleural, pericardial, and peritoneal membranes are the three main examples.
column4: A serous membrane wraps each lung, the heart, and most abdominal organs.

column2: Directional term meaning toward the head or upper part of a structure; opposite of inferior.
column4: The head is superior to the shoulders.

column2: Directional term meaning toward the front of the body; opposite of posterior.
column4: The sternum is anterior to the heart.

column2: Directional term meaning toward the midline of the body; opposite of lateral.
column4: The nose is medial to the ears.

column2: Directional term meaning closer to the point of attachment of a limb to the trunk; opposite of distal.
column4: The elbow is proximal to the wrist.

column2: Directional term meaning closer to the surface of the body; opposite of deep.
column4: The skin is superficial to the muscles beneath it.

column2: Directional term meaning on the opposite side of the body; opposite of ipsilateral.
column4: A stroke on the right side of the brain produces weakness on the contralateral (left) side of the body.

column2: The four regions (RUQ, LUQ, RLQ, LLQ) formed by one transverse and one midsagittal line through the navel, used to locate abdominal pain and organs.
column4: Appendix pain is usually reported in the RLQ, one of the four abdominopelvic quadrants.
